subject: english
learning_objectives: Define a verb and give examples
Distinguish lexical verbs from auxiliary verbs (primary and modal)
Explain finite and non-finite verbs with examples

sections.heading: Definition
sections.content:
A verb expresses action or a state of being.
Examples: sing, dance, jump, is, are, am.

sections.heading: Types of Verbs
sections.content:
Lexical (Main) Verb – expresses action; can stand on its own.
  Examples: speak, pray, write

Auxiliary Verbs – help the main verb.
  Primary Auxiliaries: be, am, is, are, was, were, being, been
    (These can also function as main verbs.)
  Modal Auxiliaries: can, could, may, might, shall, should, will, would,
    ought to, dare, need

Finite Verb – agrees with the subject in person, tense, and number.
  She works hard (present). | She worked hard (past).
  Olu speaks English (singular subject + singular verb).
  Olu and Bola speak English (plural subject + plural verb).

Non-Finite Verb – does NOT agree with the subject in person, number, or tense.
  Infinitives: to work, to eat, to dance
  -ing forms (gerunds/participles): dancing, singing, praying, cooking

Transitive Verb – requires a direct object.
  He killed a snake. [a snake = direct object of 'killed']

Intransitive Verb – does not require an object.
  She died. | They cooked. | We prayed.
